---
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# Spatial Interaction Modelling {#spatial_interaction}

## Introduction

![](images/banner-spatial-interaction.png)

### Aims {.unnumbered}

The aims of this practical are to:

1.  Understand the aims and common applications of spatial interaction (gravity) models.
2.  Understand the design and calibration of spatial interaction models.
3.  Apply spatial interaction models to a contemporary sociological issue.


### Application {.unnumbered}

To that end, we'll develop spatial interaction models to predict internal migration flows between local authorities in England and Wales. 

### Data {.unnumbered}

All data for this practical have been sourced from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) at the local authority level and are found in [`data/ons/local-authority`] as follows:

- ONS internal migration flows for local authorities in England and Wales (2021) [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/populationandmigration/populationestimates/datasets/internalmigrationinenglandandwales)]
- ONS district geometries (2021) [[Source]((https://geoportal.statistics.gov.uk/datasets/ons%3E::local-authority-districts-december-2021-boundaries-gb-bfc/explore?location=54.734360%2C-2.584695%2C7))]
- ONS gross domestic product (GDP) per head at current market prices [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/datasets/gdp-by-local-authority/editions/time-series/versions/2)]
- ONS population [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/populationandmigration/populationestimates/datasets/estimatesofthepopulationforenglandandwales)]

### Tools {.unnumbered}

[Feature To Point](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/feature-to-point.html?tabs=dialog) (Data Management Tools), [Generate Near Table](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/generate-near-table.html?tabs=dialog) (Analysis Tools), [Generalized Linear Regression](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geoanalytics-desktop/generalized-linear-regression.html?tabs=dialog) (GeoAnalytics Desktop Tools), [Notebooks in ArcGIS Pro](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/arcpy/get-started/pro-notebooks.html)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical

TO DO:

-   Find some additional datasets for the "Something more?" section
-   Update the Python notebook code to use a relative file path

--------------- Concepts:

-\> Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation -\> Spatial flows between an
origin and a destination are modelled as proportional to their
gravitational force and inversely related to their spatial separation

--------------- Sources:

-\> [!] <https://rpubs.com/adam_dennett/376877> -\> [!]
<https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10052338/7/Dennett_38-41-115-1-10-20181111.pdf>
-\> <https://openjournals.wu.ac.at/region/paper_175/175.html> -\>
<https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/simodels/vignettes/simodels.html>
-\>
<https://robinlovelace.net/simodels/articles/sims-first-principles.html>
-\>
<https://repository.ncrm.ac.uk/resources/online/all/spatial_interaction_modelling>
-\>
<https://researchbriefings.files.parliament.uk/documents/SN06077/SN06077.pdf>
-\> <https://worldmigrationreport.iom.int/msite/wmr-2024-interactive/>
-\> <https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0286244> -\>
<https://rpubs.com/adam_dennett/259068>

--------------- Data:

-\> Internal migration flows:
<https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/populationandmigration/populationestimates/datasets/internalmigrationinenglandandwales>

-\> District geometries (2021):
<https://geoportal.statistics.gov.uk/datasets/ons>::local-authority-districts-december-2021-boundaries-gb-bfc/explore?location=54.734360%2C-2.584695%2C7

-\> GDP (Gross domestic product per head at current market prices):
<https://www.ons.gov.uk/datasets/gdp-by-local-authority/editions/time-series/versions/2>

-\> Population:
<https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/populationandmigration/populationestimates/datasets/estimatesofthepopulationforenglandandwales>

--------------- Summary

(1) Produce bilateral distances and link origin-destination attributes
    for England and Wales local authority data
(2) Produce unconstrained (or total constrained) model
(3) Calibrate the model using Poisson regression
(4) Introduce production-, attraction- and doubly-constrained models
(5) Run constrained models using Python Notebook and `statsmodels` glm()
(6) Something more? Find the variables which best predict flow..



[1] Load Local Authority districts

[2] Return Centroids (Feature to Point): -\> Discuss: sensitivity to
feature geometry -\> Calculate Geometry Attributes

[3] Generate Near Table (links based on ID), using geodesic measurement,
[!] save in Geodatabase (editable)

[4] Load Gross domestic product (`gdp`) and population (`pop`) csv's and
use Join Field to add to Centroids data

[5] Join Field, keeping the origin information (IN_FID), using 'Use
Field Mapping' to select attributes of interest and updating names: -
orig_LAD21CD (unique ID) - orig_LAD21NM (name) - orig_gdp - orig_pop -
orig_long (or E) - orig_lat (or N)

[6] Repeat for destinations (NEAR_ID): - dest_LAD21CD (unique ID) -
dest_LAD21NM (name) - dest_gdp - dest_pop - dest_long (or E) - dest_lat
(or N)

[7] Create origin-destination field in the near table - !orig_LAD21CD! +
"-" + !dest_LAD21C!

[8] Load internal migration data (`flow`) and inspect - Histogram -
Min-Max

[9] Join Field, based on origin-destination field, transferring `flow`
(time required)

[10] Data cleaning: -\> Select by Attributes and remove rows: -
orig_pop, dest_pop = 0 - orig_gdp, dest_gdp = 0 - `flow` is null -\>
Result is (\<) \~80_000 rows

[11] Visualise some flow data - Select by Attributes i.e., `flow` \>
1_000 (n=\~450) - XY to Line (`ORIG_LON` - Size by `flow` [REMOVE] as a
complex dataset

--------------- Data summary

We now have all the data we need to begin modelling... similar format to
Dennett (2018): <https://rpubs.com/adam_dennett/376877>

Present data columns: -\> Origin (`orig_LAD21CD`) -\> Destination
(`dest_LAD21CD`) -\> Flow (`flow`, \# people) -\> Origin (vi) population
(`orig_pop`) -\> Destination (wj) population (`dest_pop`) -\> Origin GDP
(`orig_gdp`) -\> Destination GDP (`dest_gdp`) -\> Distance (`NEAR_DIST`,
meters)

[12] Exploratory plots - any correlations?

--------------- Model description

Classic multiplicative gravity model:

(EQ1) $T_{ij} = k \frac{V_i^μ W_j^α}{d_{ij}^{\beta}}$

Can also be written from Wilson (1971) as:

(EQ2) $T_{ij} = kV_i^μ W_j^αd_{ij}^{-\beta}$

The latter is mathematically identical, but removes the fraction
(simpler).

From Dennett:

"The flows between an origin and destination are proportional to the
product of the mass of the origin and destination and inversely
proportional to the distance between them.

As origin and destination masses increase, flows increase, but as
distance increases, flows decrease, and vice versa."

Here:

$T_{ij}$ is the predicted flow between origins and destinations. $V$ is
the origin "emissiveness" i.e., origin population and salaries (e.g.,
`ORIG_POP`, `ORIG_GDP`, `ORIG_GDP_CAP`) $W$ is the destination
"attractiveness" i.e., destination population and salaries (e.g.,
`DEST_POP`, `DEST_GDP`, `DEST_GDP_CAP`) $d$ is the cost of flows i.e.,
distance (`NEAR_DIST`) $k$, $μ$, $α$, $\beta$ are model parameters to be
estimated

These parameters relate to the scaling effect or importance of the
variables they are associated with.

We can start with some basic parameters i.e., effects scale in a linear
fashion. For a 1 unit increase in population, we might expect a 1 unit
increase in flows of people from that origin.

Newtons gravity uses a power law of $\beta=-2$, so here are the starting
values:

$k=1$, $μ=1$, $α=1$, $\beta=-2$

For $V$ (origin "emissiveness") we will use origin population
(`orig_pop`) For $W$ (destination "attractiveness"), we will use
destination GDP per capita (`dest_gdp`) For $d$, we use $d^\beta$, where
$d$ = `NEAR_DIST` and $\beta=-2$

`gm1` = $V$ \* $W$ \* $d$

--------------- Modelling

[13] Add Field (`gm1`), Float, following equation (distance in km)

pow(!orig_pop!, 1) \* pow(!dest_gdp!, 1) \* pow(!NEAR_DIST!, 1000, -2)

This produces an unconstrained output (unitless), as we have not used
the k factor, which converts our model to a *total constrained* version
by scaling to match the total number of flows i.e., sum of all observed
flows divided by the sum of all of the other elements in the model.

[14] Using Explore Statistics, return the sum of `flow`
(102228926.73000628) and `gm1` (3912.65851170132), and then calculate a
new field incorporating the scale factor (`gm1_scale`)

Data = 3698001.8812999986 (\# internal migrations) Model =
\~63224.55841802744 (sum of `gm1`)

!gm1! \* (3698001.8812999986 / 63224.55841802744)

[!] Note: we can't do this all via Add Field, as this is a row-wise
calculation

[15] Model evaluation

First, check the total of `gm1_scale` matches the total flow (it does!),
so our model is *total constrained*

[!] However, the output is pretty abysmal here (R2=0.07, Create Chart,
Scatter Plot), because we haven't tuned our parameters!

--------------- Tuning parameters

If we log both sides of the equation above, we get the following:

(EQ3)
$\mathrm{ln}T_{ij}=k+μ\mathrm{ln}V_i+α\mathrm{ln}W_j−β\mathrm{ln}d_{ij}$

This is basically a regression model!

However, there are problems with using a log-linear approach, and
instead we use Poisson regression, which is better for skewed
distributions (like our flow data)

[16] Visualise Statistics - Histrogram - Flow

So our Poisson regression looks like the following:

(EQ4)
$λ_{ij}=\mathrm{exp}(k+μ\mathrm{ln}V_i+α\mathrm{ln}W_j−β\mathrm{ln}d_{ij})$

[17] First, log the independent variables using Add Field -\>
math.log(!orig_pop!) -\> math.log(!dest_gdp!) -\> math.log(!NEAR_DIST! /
1000) \# converting to km -\> \* see section below on math.log()

[18] Run Generalized Linear Regression (GeoAnalytics Desktop Tools) -\>
Must use GDT version, as this allows for table inputs -\> Flow
(dependent) -\> Logged variables (independent) -\> Count (Poisson)

[19] Evaluate and interpret coefficients

Variable -------- Coefficients ---- Standard error ---- t-Statistic ----
Probability orig_pop_log ---- 0.9985 ---------- 0.0009 -----------
1071.6299 ------ 0 dest_gdp_log ---- 0.2862 ---------- 0.0006
----------- 446.6138 ------- 0 near_dist_log --- -1.2354 ---------
0.0005 ----------- -2611.7296 ----- 0 Intercept ------- -5.4022
--------- 0.0132 ----------- -409.3151 ------ 0

[20] Produce `gm2` (calibrated model) and evaluate performance using
Create Chart, Scatter Plot

Equation:
math.exp(-5.4022+(0.9985*!orig_pop_log!)+(0.2862*!dest_gdp_log!)-(abs(-1.2354)\*!near_dist_log!))

Performance: R2 = 0.45

[21] Introduce Origin constrained models

-\> This is technically possible in ArcGIS but not easy, requiring us to
use Encode Field to create dummy variables for `orig_LAD21CD`. -\>
Generalised Linear Regression (tool) struggles with this number of
variables (\~300) with multicollinearity issues -\> Also very difficult
to manually apply the coefficients to a regression equations

-\> Instead, load a Python notebook and use the appendix code to perform
GLR and predict (`gm3`s), saving output to csv

[\*] Important, while I don't expect the students to write the code
independently, I should introduce it in a peicemeal fashion, explaining
what each line does.

[22] Load predictions csv and assess performance (`flow_int` vs. `gm3`):

-\> R2 = 0.57 -\> Sum of modelled flows for each origin matches measured
flows

[23] Discuss destination-constrained and doubly-constrained models

[\*] Ask students to run a destination-constrained model, adapting the
Python code to produce `gm4`?

## Extra

Options:

-\> Add additional predictors e.g., median earnings rather than GDP,
unemployment rate, housing affordability, share of working-age
population

```         
- We can do this for the unconstrained, production or attraction constrained models (i.e., more predictors for the origin or destination). but not for the doubly-constrained model, as this takes into account the distance only (?). 
```

-\> Include contiguity: - Create numeric ID for LA geometries using
SequentialNumber() - Create Spatial Weights Matrix (Contiguity Edges
Corners) - Convert Spatial Weights Matrix to Table - Join SWM table to
LA geometries: = NUMERIC_ID (origin) to numeric_ID: orig_LAD21CD = NID
(destination) to numeric_ID: dest_LAD21CD - Create origin-destination
field - Create binary bordering field (`contig` = 1) - Join Field:
NEAR_TABLE to SVWM based on origin-destination, linking `contig` = This
produces 1 (bordering) or <Null> (not bordering) - Calculate Field,
update `contig` using: 0 if !contig! is None else !contig! - Add
contiguity to the model

--------------- Log issues

Python: math.log() uses `e` as the default

R: log() uses `exp(1)` as the default, which is identical to above:
<https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/base/versions/3.6.2/topics/log>

Excel: log() defaults to base10, which is different! Either plug in the
above i.e., log(val, EXP(1)), or use LN()

--------------- Python Notebook for GLR Poisson

```         
import os
import arcpy
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

# Current ArcGIS Pro project
aprx = arcpy.mp.ArcGISProject("CURRENT")
# List maps
m = aprx.listMaps()[0]

# Access table
table = m.listTables("la_near_table_v2")[0]
table_path = table.dataSource
workspace = arcpy.Describe(ta

# List of fields
fields = [f.name for f in arcpy.ListFields(table_path)]

# Data to pd
rows = arcpy.da.SearchCursor(table_path, fields)
df = pd.DataFrame(list(rows), columns=fields)

# Create formula and run glm 
model_formula = "flow_int ~ C(ORIG_LAD21CD) + orig_pop_log + dest_gdp_log + near_dist_log - 1"
model = smf.glm(formula=model_formula, data=df, family=sm.families.Poisson()).fit()

# Summary
# print(model.summary())

# Predictions
df["gm3"] = model.predict(exog=df)

# Sets path to output (workspace + file name)
#out_table = os.path.join(workspace, "la_near_table_v2_pred.csv")

# Manual export to csv
df.to_csv(r"C:/Users/mzdi2mt2/Documents/GEOG73001-processing/week-10/la_near_table_v2_pred.csv", index=False)
```

## Resources

- Wilson, A.G. ([1971](https://doi.org/10.1068/a030001)). A family of spatial interaction models, and associated developments. Environment and Planning A, 3(1), pp.1-32.
- Haynes, K.E., & Fotheringham, A.S. ([1985](https://researchrepository.wvu.edu/rri-web-book/16/)). Gravity and Spatial Interaction Models. Reprint. Edited by Grant Ian Thrall. WVU Research Repository, 2020.
- Fotheringham, A.S. and M.E. O’Kelly ([1989](https://www.amazon.co.uk/Spatial-Interaction-Models-Formulations-Applications/dp/0792300211)) Spatial Interaction Models: Formulations and Applications. London: Kluwer Academic.
- Dennett, A. and Wilson, A. ([2013](https://doi.org/10.1068/a45398)). A multilevel spatial interaction modelling framework for estimating interregional migration in Europe. Environment and Planning A, 45(6), pp.1491-1507.
- Rowe, F., Lovelace, R. and Dennett, A. ([2024](https://doi.org/10.4337/9781802203233.00019)). Spatial interaction modelling: a manifesto. In A research agenda for spatial analysis (pp. 177-196). Edward Elgar Publishing.
- Liao, M. and Oshan, T.M. ([2025](https://doi.org/10.1111/gean.70001)). A Data‐Driven Approach to Spatial Interaction Models of Migration: Integrating and Refining the Theories of Competing Destinations and Intervening Opportunities. Geographical Analysis, 57(3), pp.540-554.
